disease.name: Generalized Peritonitis
disease.note: Inflammation of the peritoneal cavity caused by infection (usually from a perforated viscus), chemical irritation, or inflammation. A surgical syndrome rather than a single disease — the key is identifying and controlling the source. Secondary peritonitis from a perforated viscus (appendix, ulcer, diverticulitis, typhoid) is most common in our setting. Tertiary peritonitis is increasingly seen in ICU patients with resistant organisms. Mortality ranges from 5% (community-acquired, early) to >50% (hospital-acquired, septic shock).
presentingComplaints: Abdominal pain that started in one spot and spread
Unable to pass stool or gas

history_questions.question: Where did the pain start and did it spread to the whole abdomen?
When did the pain start? Was it sudden or gradual?
Have you had fever with chills and rigors?
Have you had any recent abdominal surgery or procedure?
Have you had any injury or trauma to your abdomen?
Have you had a known ulcer, gallstones, or appendicitis in the past?
Have you been having abdominal pain in the days before this severe episode?
Do you have liver disease or cirrhosis? Do you have swelling in your legs or abdomen (ascites)?
Are you on peritoneal dialysis (fluid in the belly to clean your blood)?
Have you been taking NSAIDs (ibuprofen, diclofenac) or steroids?
Do you have diabetes, high blood pressure, or HIV?
Have you been treated for typhoid fever in the last 2-3 weeks?
Have you vomited? What colour is the vomit?
When did you last pass stool and gas?
Do you feel short of breath or have difficulty breathing?
Have you noticed a decrease in urine output?
Do you feel dizzy or light-headed when you try to sit up?
Have you been on antibiotics recently?
Have you had weight loss, night sweats, or chronic cough? (suggests TB peritonitis or abdominal TB)
Have you ever tested positive for HIV or been on antiretroviral therapy?
For women: are you pregnant or could you be? When was your last menstrual period? Any vaginal discharge or bleeding?
Have you had any recent endoscopic procedures (gastroscopy, colonoscopy, ERCP)?
Do you have a known hernia (groin, umbilical, or incisional)? Is it painful or stuck?
Have you had any previous abdominal surgeries? If so, when and what type?
Do you have a history of diverticulosis, inflammatory bowel disease (Crohn's, UC), or colon polyps?
Have you been diagnosed with any cancer, especially of the stomach, colon, ovary, or pancreas?
Do you smoke cigarettes or use any other tobacco products?
Do you drink alcohol? How much and how often?
Have you been on any medications that weaken the immune system (chemotherapy, steroids, immunosuppressants)?
Have you had any recent travel or contact with someone who is sick with diarrhoea or fever?
Have you eaten any food that may have been contaminated or unpasteurised dairy products?
Do you have any pain in your shoulder tip? (suggests diaphragmatic irritation from subphrenic collection)
Have you felt feverish with chills and drenching night sweats?
Are you currently on peritoneal dialysis? When was your last exchange? Any cloudiness of the fluid?
Have you had any recent trauma or injury to your abdomen?
Have you experienced any bleeding from the back passage (haematochezia, melena) or blood in vomit?

examination_findings.finding: Generalized abdominal tenderness (all four quadrants)
Rigidity (board-like) of the entire abdominal wall
Rebound tenderness (diffuse)
Guarding (voluntary and involuntary)
Absent bowel sounds (silent abdomen)
Abdominal distension
Liver dullness obliterated on percussion (if free air)
Percussion tenderness (even gentle percussion causes pain)
Tachycardia (pulse >100 bpm)
Hypotension (systolic BP <90 mmHg)
Fever (temperature >38°C) — may be hypothermic in severe sepsis
Tachypnea (respiratory rate >20/min)
Hypoxia (oxygen saturation <95% on room air)
Cool, clammy, mottled extremities (poor perfusion)
Oliguria (urine output <0.5 mL/kg/hr)
Altered mental state (confusion, agitation, or drowsiness — sign of sepsis)
Patient lies absolutely still, prefers to lie with knees flexed (peritoneal irritation)
Shallow, grunting respirations (splinting of diaphragm)
Tender on DRE in all quadrants (pelvic peritonitis)
Purulent, bilious, or feculent fluid on paracentesis
qSOFA ≥2: altered mental state + RR >22/min + SBP <100 mmHg
Generalized abdominal distension with tympany to percussion
Obliteration of liver dullness (gas in peritoneal cavity)
Percussion tenderness — even gentle fingertip percussion over the abdomen is exquisitely painful
Cough tenderness — cough causes sharp pain at the site of peritoneal irritation (Dunphy's sign)
Shallow, grunting respirations — diaphragmatic splinting from peritoneal inflammation
Patient lies completely still with knees drawn up (flexed hips reduce peritoneal tension)
Involuntary guarding progressing to true board-like rigidity
Tenderness on gentle pelvic (DRE) or vaginal examination — indicates pelvic peritonitis or abscess
Capillary refill time >3 seconds (poor peripheral perfusion from distributive shock)
Dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes, reduced skin turgor (dehydration and third spacing)
Mottled or cool extremities (peripheral vasoconstriction from catecholamine surge)
Confusion, agitation, obtundation, or GCS <15 (sepsis-associated encephalopathy)
Jaundice (suggests biliary source, hepatic dysfunction, or haemolysis)
Ascites detectable by shifting dullness or fluid thrill (in SBP or advanced peritonitis)
Hernial orifices — examine carefully for incarcerated/strangulated hernia causing peritonitis
Abdominal wall cellulitis or necrotizing fasciitis (rare — gas-forming organisms)
Positive psoas sign (pain on passive hip extension) suggests retroperitoneal inflammation
Positive obturator sign (pain on passive internal rotation of flexed hip) suggests pelvic inflammation

bedside_scores.name: qSOFA Score (quick Sequential Organ Failure Assessment)

interpretation.low: qSOFA 0-1: Low risk of sepsis-related mortality. Nevertheless, peritonitis requires source control.
interpretation.moderate: qSOFA 2: Moderate risk. High suspicion of sepsis. ICU admission strongly recommended.
interpretation.high: qSOFA 3: High risk of poor outcome. Likely septic shock. Aggressive resuscitation + emergency source control.

interpretation.low: Score <21: Low risk. Mortality <2%. Laparotomy and source control.
interpretation.moderate: Score 21-29: Moderate risk. Mortality ~10-15%. ICU care.
interpretation.high: Score >29: High risk. Mortality >50%. Damage control surgery. Aggressive ICU management.

riskFactors.note: Appendicitis, diverticulitis, ulcer, typhoid perforation — most common causes
Anastomotic leak, missed injury, contamination at index operation
Impaired wound healing and immune function
Risk of SBP (primary peritonitis in ascites). Worse surgical outcomes.
Direct route of infection into peritoneal cavity (PD peritonitis)
Atypical presentation, higher morbidity and mortality

subtypes.name: Secondary Peritonitis (Perforated Viscus)
subtypes.management: Emergency laparotomy. Source control: repair/resection of perforated viscus, appendicectomy, etc. Thorough peritoneal lavage (5-10 L warm saline until returning fluid is clear). Culture peritoneal fluid. IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (Piperacillin/Tazobactam 4.5 g 6-hourly or Meropenem 1 g 8-hourly). ICU admission. Directed antibiotics based on culture results. Delayed primary skin closure if gross contamination.

subtypes.name: Primary (Spontaneous Bacterial) Peritonitis
subtypes.management: Medical management — NO SURGERY. IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (third-generation cephalosporin — Ceftriaxone 2 g daily for 5-7 days). IV albumin if renal impairment. Treat underlying liver disease. Secondary prophylaxis with norfloxacin or ciprofloxacin. Diagnostic paracentesis mandatory for confirmation.

subtypes.name: Tertiary Peritonitis (Persistent / Recurrent)
subtypes.management: Re-laparotomy. Culture-directed antimicrobial therapy. Source control re-assessment. Open abdomen management with negative pressure therapy (Barker's vacuum pack or commercial system). Nutritional support (enteral + parenteral). Anti-fungal therapy (fluconazole or echinocandin). MDT approach with microbiology and infectious disease.

subtypes.name: Tuberculous Peritonitis
subtypes.management: Medical management — NO SURGERY unless complications (obstruction, perforation). Diagnostic paracentesis for ADA, TB GeneXpert, and culture. Laparoscopy with peritoneal biopsy (most sensitive — granulomas on histology in 90%). Start 4-drug anti-TB therapy (Rifampicin, Isoniazid, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol) for 2 months followed by 4 months of Rifampicin and Isoniazid. Monitor for paradoxical reaction on treatment. If ascites tense: therapeutic paracentesis. Corticosteroids may reduce adhesion formation in severe cases.

mimics.whyMimic: Severe abdominal pain, vomiting, peritonism, can be indistinguishable from peritonitis
Generalized peritonitis from infarcted bowel mimics secondary peritonitis
Abdominal pain, vomiting, tenderness, guarding can mimic peritonitis in DKA
Severe abdominal pain, vomiting, distension, can mimic acute abdomen in sickle cell patients in East Africa
Common cause of peritonitis in East Africa, presents with generalized peritonitis indistinguishable from other causes
Diffuse abdominal pain, vomiting, fever, distension can mimic peritonitis, especially in children
Sudden severe lower abdominal pain, peritonism, haemorrhagic shock mimics generalized peritonitis in women of reproductive age
Abdominal pain, distension, ileus, shock from retroperitoneal bleeding can mimic peritonitis

mimics.diseaseName: Lower Lobe Pneumonia with Diaphragmatic Pleurisy
mimics.whyMimic: Upper abdominal pain, guarding, fever from diaphragmatic pleural irritation can be mistaken for peritonitis

bedside.name: qSOFA Score
bedside.indication: Assess sepsis severity: RR >22, altered mental state, SBP <100. qSOFA ≥2 strongly suggests sepsis.

bedside.name: Erect Chest X-Ray
bedside.indication: Free air under diaphragm suggests perforated viscus as cause of peritonitis.

bedside.name: Diagnostic Peritoneal Aspiration (Paracentesis)
bedside.indication: If diagnosis uncertain and imaging unavailable. Pus, bile, feculent fluid, or blood confirms peritonitis. Send fluid for culture, Gram stain, WBC count, and biochemistry (amylase, bilirubin, creatinine).

bedside.name: Urinary catheter
bedside.indication: Monitor urine output as index of organ perfusion

bedside.name: NG tube insertion
bedside.indication: Gastric decompression. Aspirate may show blood, bilious, or feculent content.

laboratory.name: WBC count
laboratory.indication: Leukocytosis typical. Leukopenia (<4,000) suggests severe sepsis and poor prognosis.

laboratory.name: CRP
laboratory.indication: Markedly elevated in peritonitis. >200 suggests severe infection.

laboratory.name: Procalcitonin
laboratory.indication: Differentiates bacterial peritonitis from sterile inflammation. >2 ng/mL suggests bacterial infection.

laboratory.name: Lactate
laboratory.indication: >2 mmol/L suggests tissue hypoperfusion. >4 suggests severe sepsis/shock.

laboratory.name: Arterial Blood Gas
laboratory.indication: Metabolic acidosis (low pH, low HCO3, negative BE) suggests shock and hypoperfusion.

laboratory.name: Blood cultures ×2
laboratory.indication: Positive in 20-40% of secondary peritonitis. Gram-negative rods and anaerobes common.

laboratory.name: Urea and Creatinine
laboratory.indication: AKI common in peritonitis from hypovolemia and sepsis.

laboratory.name: Electrolytes (Na, K, Cl, HCO3)
laboratory.indication: Metabolic disturbances from vomiting and third spacing.

laboratory.name: Liver Function Tests
laboratory.indication: Elevated transaminases in shock liver. Elevated bilirubin suggests biliary cause.

laboratory.name: Serum Amylase / Lipase
laboratory.indication: Rule out acute pancreatitis as cause.

laboratory.name: Blood Glucose
laboratory.indication: Stress hyperglycemia common. Rule out DKA as mimic (check ketones).

laboratory.name: Coagulation profile (INR, APTT)
laboratory.indication: DIC common in prolonged sepsis. Pre-operative assessment.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen with IV contrast
imaging.indication: Gold standard. Identifies: free air, free fluid, site of perforation, abscess, bowel wall thickening, mesenteric changes. Helps identify cause and guide surgical approach.

imaging.name: Erect Chest X-Ray
imaging.indication: Detect free air under diaphragm. First-line in suspected perforation.

imaging.name: Abdominal X-Ray (supine + erect)
imaging.indication: May show free air (Rigler's sign), air-fluid levels (ileus), bowel dilatation.

imaging.name: Abdominal Ultrasound
imaging.indication: Can detect free fluid, abscess, gallstones, appendicitis. Limited by gas. Useful in children and pregnancy. Can guide paracentesis.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen without contrast
imaging.indication: If contrast contraindicated (renal failure, allergy). Can still detect free air, fluid, and some pathology.

management.protocolReference: WSES Guidelines for the Management of Intra-Abdominal Infections (2022) / Surviving Sepsis Campaign

resuscitation: High-flow oxygen via non-rebreather mask — target SpO2 >95%
Large bore IV access ×2 (14G or 16G). Central line if vasopressors needed.
Goal-directed fluid resuscitation: Hartmann's or Normal Saline 30 mL/kg bolus within first 3 hours, repeat as needed
Vasopressors (Noradrenaline first-line) if MAP <65 mmHg despite adequate fluid loading
NG tube — free drainage, gastric decompression
Urinary catheter — hourly urine output, target >0.5 mL/kg/hr
IV broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour of diagnosis: Piperacillin/Tazobactam 4.5 g 6-hourly OR Meropenem 1 g 8-hourly
Blood cultures ×2 before antibiotics (do not delay antibiotics if cultures not possible)
Source control: emergency laparotomy as soon as patient is resuscitated (target <2 hours from decision)
Type and crossmatch — 4 units PRBC on standby
Consent for emergency laparotomy — including stoma possibility and open abdomen
conservative: Not appropriate for established secondary peritonitis — surgery is mandatory
For SBP (primary peritonitis): IV antibiotics alone, NO surgery
For contained abscess with minimal peritonitis: antibiotics + percutaneous drainage, close monitoring

surgical: Emergency laparotomy — midline, full length for complete exploration
Identify and control the source: repair perforation, resect perforated viscus, drain abscess
Thorough peritoneal lavage: 5-10 L warm saline until returning fluid is clear — remove all purulent/feculent material
Culture peritoneal fluid from multiple quadrants
Assess need for bowel resection: gangrenous bowel, tumor, necrotic tissue
Primary anastomosis: only if haemodynamically stable, minimal contamination, healthy bowel ends
Stoma (ileostomy/colostomy): if severe contamination, unstable patient, multiple resections, or preoperative shock

Open abdomen (temporary abdominal closure): for severe peritonitis with: gross contamination not controllable, planned second-look for bowel viability, abdominal compartment syndrome, damage control in unstable patient
Drain placement: in pelvis and affected quadrants if ongoing contamination expected
nonOperative: IV antibiotics alone — for SBP only
Percutaneous drainage of well-defined abscess with localized peritonitis
Resuscitation and organ support in ICU

immediate: ICU admission mandatory for all but mildest cases
Continue IV broad-spectrum antibiotics — tailor based on peritoneal culture results
Vasoactive drugs as needed — wean as tolerated
VTE prophylaxis — SC enoxaparin 40 mg daily (start when coagulation stable)
Pain control: IV Paracetamol + Morphine PCA
Monitor drain output: volume, colour, character
Check daily: WBC, CRP, lactate, ABG, U+E, LFTs
day1_3: Continue ICU care — wean vasopressors, vent weaning
Monitor for ongoing sepsis: persistent fever, rising WBC, high vasopressor requirement → suspect missed source/residual infection
If open abdomen: manage temporary closure, plan staged closure or second-look at 24-72h
Enteral nutrition: start NG/NJ feeds within 24-48h if haemodynamically stable
Remove NG tube when output <500 mL/day and bowel sounds return
day3_7: Transition to ward if haemodynamically stable, no vasopressors, extubated
Tailor antibiotics based on culture results
Wound care — staged closure for open abdomen
Plan discharge: when afebrile 48h, tolerating diet, pain controlled on oral analgesia

pathways.criteria: Localized peritonitis (e.g., contained abscess), no organ failure, qSOFA 0-1, MPI <21, lactate <2
pathways.treatment: IV antibiotics. Source control (percutaneous drainage or laparoscopic surgery). Peritoneal lavage. Ward or HDU care. Antibiotics 5-7 days.

pathways.criteria: Generalized peritonitis, early sepsis, qSOFA 1-2, MPI 21-29, lactate 2-4, pre-op duration <24h
pathways.treatment: Emergency laparotomy. Source control + lavage. ICU admission. IV broad-spectrum antibiotics 7-10 days. Enteral nutrition. Monitor for organ failure.

pathways.criteria: Generalized peritonitis, septic shock, qSOFA 3, MPI >29, lactate >4, organ failure, pre-op duration >24h, immunosuppressed
pathways.treatment: Goal-directed resuscitation (fluids, vasopressors, antibiotics within 1h). Damage control laparotomy: rapid source control, temporary abdominal closure, ICU resuscitation. Second-look laparotomy at 24-48h. Definitive closure after resolution. Prolonged antibiotics (10-14+ days). ICU care.

laparotomy_source_control.title: Emergency Laparotomy for Source Control in Generalized Peritonitis
laparotomy_source_control.postOpDiagnosis: Generalized Secondary Peritonitis due to [cause identified]
laparotomy_source_control.procedure: Midline laparotomy, source control, peritoneal lavage, ± drain placement, ± temporary abdominal closure
laparotomy_source_control.indication: Generalized peritonitis requiring source identification and control
laparotomy_source_control.incision: Midline laparotomy from xiphisternum to pubis
steps: Full midline laparotomy — careful entry to avoid injured adherent bowel
Note the character of peritoneal fluid on entry: serous, purulent, bilious, feculent, or bloody — take culture swabs from multiple quadrants
Systematic abdominal exploration:
Right upper quadrant: gallbladder, duodenum, liver, hepatic flexure
Right lower quadrant: appendix, cecum, terminal ileum
Left upper quadrant: stomach, splenic flexure, spleen
Left lower quadrant: sigmoid colon
Pelvis: small bowel loops, bladder, uterus/adnexa in women
Identify the source of contamination:
Perforated appendix → appendicectomy
Perforated duodenal ulcer → Graham patch
Perforated gastric ulcer → wedge excision + closure
Typhoid/TB ileal perforation → primary closure or resection + anastomosis
Perforated diverticulitis → Hartmann procedure or resection + anastomosis + protecting ileostomy
Gangrenous bowel → resection
Anastomotic leak → repair or re-fashion anastomosis
Ischemic bowel → resection, no anastomosis at index surgery
Once source controlled: copious peritoneal lavage with warm saline (minimum 5-10 L) until effluent clear
Place drains: pelvis, subphrenic spaces, and paracolic gutters as indicated
Decide on bowel continuity: primary anastomosis vs stoma vs damage control
Decide on abdominal closure: primary closure vs temporary closure (if: planned second-look, abdominal compartment syndrome risk, gross edema, contamination uncontrolled)
For temporary closure: negative pressure wound therapy (Barker's vacuum pack) or Bogota bag
For primary closure: mass closure with PDS loop ± retention sutures if high risk
Skin: leave open (delayed primary closure) if gross contamination; close loosely if minimal
keyLandmarks: All four quadrants must be visualised and explored
Retroperitoneum: duodenum, pancreas, kidneys, ureters — assess if pathology suggested
Lesser sac: enter via gastrocolic ligament if pancreatic pathology suspected
Root of mesentery: assess SMA pulse for ischemia

laparoscopic_lavage.title: Diagnostic Laparoscopy ± Lavage for Peritonitis
laparoscopic_lavage.procedure: Diagnostic laparoscopy, source control, peritoneal lavage
laparoscopic_lavage.indication: Hemodynamically stable patient with suspected peritonitis where source not clearly identified on imaging
laparoscopic_lavage.incision: Three-port technique: umbilical (camera), working ports placed based on suspected pathology
steps: Hassan technique at umbilicus (safer in distended abdomen or with bowel adhesions)
Insufflate to 12 mmHg, introduce 30° scope
Insert working ports under vision
Systematic visualization of all quadrants
Identify the source of peritonitis
If appendicitis: laparoscopic appendicectomy
If perforated ulcer: laparoscopic Graham patch repair (if expertise available)
If diverticulitis with abscess: laparoscopic drainage
Irrigate and suction all contaminated fluid
Place drain if indicated
Remove ports under vision, close port sites
keyLandmarks: As per underlying cause — see specific templates

immediate_postop_24h.complication: Persistent sepsis from incomplete source control

postop_day1_3.complication: Open abdomen complications (fluid losses, bowel desiccation)

watchFor: failure to tolerate enteral feeds by day 7
persistent discharge from wound or drain site

late.complication: Chronic abdominal pain / neuropathic pain syndrome

dischargeCriteria: Tolerating full soft diet without vomiting or significant distension
WBC <12,000 and trending down, CRP improving
Wound clean, dry, no signs of infection or managed with plan for closure
If stoma created: functioning, patient/carer confident with care
If drain in situ: output <30 mL/day, non-purulent, plan for removal

patient_education.diagnosis_explanation: Peritonitis is inflammation of the lining of your abdominal cavity (the peritoneum). It is usually caused by bacteria entering the abdomen from a hole (perforation) in the bowel, appendix, or stomach — or as a complication of abdominal surgery. The infection causes severe pain, fever, and if not treated, can lead to overwhelming sepsis and organ failure. Treatment involves emergency surgery to find and repair the source of the leak, wash out the infected fluid, and give strong antibiotics.
warning_signs: Abdomen becoming swollen, tense, or hard
Vomiting that prevents eating or drinking
Not passing stool or gas for more than 24 hours after discharge
Feeling faint, dizzy, or short of breath
Wound redness, pus, bleeding, or separation
If you have a stoma: stoma becomes dark/black; no output for >12 hours; excessive bleeding
patient_education.activity_advice: You have had major abdominal surgery for a severe infection — recovery will take time. Rest for at least 2 weeks after discharge. Gentle walking is encouraged. Do not lift heavy objects (>5 kg) for 6-8 weeks. Return to work: light desk work in 2-4 weeks, manual labour in 6-12 weeks. If you have a stoma, avoid contact sports until cleared.
patient_education.diet_advice: Start with small, frequent meals. Eat soft, easily digestible foods: porridge (uji), soup, rice, bread, mashed potatoes, ripe bananas, and yoghurt. Chew food well. Gradually add normal foods over 2-4 weeks. Drink plenty of water (8-10 glasses daily). If you had a bowel resection or stoma, you may need dietary adjustments — the dietitian will guide you.
patient_education.medication_advice: Complete the full course of antibiotics as prescribed — do not stop early. Take pain medication as needed (Paracetamol is safe). Avoid NSAIDs (ibuprofen, diclofenac). If you are discharged on anticoagulation (blood thinners), take them exactly as prescribed.
patient_education.follow_up_advice: Return to surgical clinic in 1-2 weeks for wound check and suture removal. The cause of your peritonitis will determine further follow-up: if gallbladder disease, arrange cholecystectomy; if typhoid, complete treatment; if TB, start anti-TB therapy; if diverticulitis or cancer, plan interval imaging. If you have a stoma, you will receive stoma care training and a reversal plan.

before_discharge: Wound care education and dressing change plan
Dietary progression plan (oral intake → soft → full diet)
Pain management plan: oral analgesia prescription
Activity restrictions: no heavy lifting 6 weeks
If stoma created: stoma care teaching, bag supply, stoma nurse referral
If drain in situ: drain care education, measuring output, plan for removal
Follow-up appointment in surgical clinic (1-2 weeks)

after_discharge.1_week: Surgical clinic review — wound inspection, suture removal, histology results discussion. Check for signs of ongoing sepsis (fever, pain, wound issues). Review drain output if applicable.
after_discharge.2_weeks: Clinical review — ensure adequate oral intake, bowel function returned, pain controlled. If TB: ensure anti-TB drugs started. If stoma: stoma therapist assessment.
after_discharge.6_weeks: Surgical clinic — assess full recovery, return to activities. Plan stoma reversal if applicable. If malignancy found: oncology referral. If conservative management alone: discharge from follow-up if asymptomatic.
after_discharge.3_months: Only if stoma created: plan reversal surgery (bowel continuity restoration). Colonoscopy or water-soluble enema before reversal.

when_to_return: Abdomen becoming swollen, hard, or tense
Vomiting that prevents you from keeping down fluids for more than 12 hours
Not passing stool or gas for more than 24 hours after discharge
Wound: redness, pus, bleeding, or separation
Enteric contents (stool, digested food) coming from wound or drain
Stoma: dark/black colour; no output for >12 hours; excessive bleeding; retraction
Shortness of breath, chest pain, or leg swelling

pathophysiologyDetail: The peritoneal cavity is the largest serosal space in the body, lined by a single layer of mesothelial cells covering a surface area of 1.7-2.0 m². The mesothelium rests on a basement membrane and submesothelial connective tissue rich in blood and lymphatic vessels. The lymphatic drainage occurs primarily via stomata in the diaphragmatic peritoneum connecting to subdiaphragmatic lymphatics and then to the thoracic duct — this is the primary route for clearance of bacteria and particulates from the peritoneal cavity.

Bacterial contamination of the peritoneal cavity triggers an immediate innate immune response. Resident peritoneal macrophages recognize pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs) — lipopolysaccharide (LPS) from Gram-negative bacteria, lipoteichoic acid from Gram-positives, and peptidoglycan — via Toll-like receptors (TLR2, TLR4, TLR5) and NOD-like receptors (NOD1, NOD2). Ligation of these receptors activates the NF-κB and MAP kinase pathways, driving the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines.

The initial response is dominated by TNF-α and IL-1β, which act synergistically to: (1) increase vascular permeability through upregulation of endothelial adhesion molecules (E-selectin, ICAM-1, VCAM-1), (2) activate the complement cascade (C3a, C5a are potent chemoattractants), (3) stimulate production of IL-8 (CXCL8), the primary neutrophil chemoattractant, and (4) induce the acute phase response via IL-6 secretion.

Neutrophil recruitment is a multi-step process: rolling on activated endothelium (mediated by selectins), firm adhesion (ICAM-1/β2 integrin interaction), and transmigration through endothelial junctions into the peritoneal cavity. Once in the peritoneal cavity, neutrophils phagocytose bacteria and release reactive oxygen species (superoxide, hydrogen peroxide) and proteolytic enzymes (elastase, cathepsin G, matrix metalloproteinases — MMP-8, MMP-9).

The complement cascade amplifies the inflammatory response. C3a and C5a (anaphylatoxins) increase vascular permeability and smooth muscle contraction. C5a is the most potent neutrophil chemoattractant. C3b opsonizes bacteria, enhancing phagocytosis. The membrane attack complex (C5b-C9) directly lyses Gram-negative bacteria. Complement activation is a double-edged sword — excessive activation contributes to tissue damage and capillary leak.

Fibrin deposition is a critical host defence mechanism. Tissue factor released from activated mesothelial cells triggers the coagulation cascade, producing a fibrin mesh that traps bacteria and limits their spread. This is the basis for the 'containment' phase of peritonitis. The fibrin mesh also acts as a scaffold for fibroblast infiltration and adhesion formation. However, excessive fibrin deposition with impaired fibrinolysis (from elevated PAI-1) leads to abscess formation and adhesive bowel obstruction.

The mesothelium itself is not a passive barrier but an active participant. Mesothelial cells express TLRs and, when activated, secrete: (1) chemokines (IL-8, MCP-1, RANTES) that recruit leukocytes, (2) prostaglandins (PGE2) that contribute to pain and vasodilation, (3) nitric oxide via iNOS induction, and (4) matrix metalloproteinases that remodel the peritoneal matrix. Damaged mesothelial cells undergo mesothelial-to-mesenchymal transition (MMT), contributing to peritoneal fibrosis in the chronic phase.

Failure of containment leads to diffuse peritonitis. The transition from localized to generalized peritonitis occurs when: (1) the bacterial inoculum overwhelms local defence mechanisms, (2) fibrinolytic activity degrades the protective fibrin mesh, (3) ongoing contamination from the source (e.g., ongoing leak from perforation) exceeds the capacity for clearance, or (4) host immunity is impaired (malnutrition, HIV, diabetes, steroids).

Systemic dissemination occurs via two routes: (1) haematogenous — bacteria and endotoxin are absorbed through mesenteric and portal circulation, reaching the liver where Kupffer cells attempt clearance but release more cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6) into the systemic circulation; (2) lymphatic — bacteria and cytokines are absorbed through diaphragmatic lymphatics directly into the systemic circulation, bypassing the hepatic filter. The lymphatic route is the most important pathway for systemic spread.

The systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) is driven by the systemic spillover of TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, HMGB1, and MIF (macrophage migration inhibitory factor). These mediators cause: (1) myocardial depression (via TNF-α and IL-1β directly depressing cardiomyocyte contractility), (2) vasoplegia (via nitric oxide-mediated vascular smooth muscle relaxation), (3) endothelial barrier dysfunction leading to capillary leak, (4) activation of the coagulation cascade with microvascular thrombosis, and (5) activation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis and the sympathetic nervous system.

Progression to septic shock and multi-organ dysfunction represents the final common pathway. Tissue hypoperfusion from distributive shock leads to: (1) lactic acidosis from anaerobic metabolism, (2) acute kidney injury from prerenal azotaemia and acute tubular necrosis, (3) acute respiratory distress syndrome from neutrophil-mediated lung injury, (4) hepatic dysfunction from ischaemic hepatitis, (5) disseminated intravascular coagulation from consumption of clotting factors, and (6) sepsis-associated encephalopathy from cerebral hypoperfusion and neurotransmitter dysregulation.

In East Africa, the microbiology of peritonitis differs from Western cohorts. Gram-negative organisms (E. coli, Klebsiella, Proteus) predominate, but anaerobes (Bacteroides fragilis, Clostridium perfringens) are present in mixed infections. Enterococcus species are increasingly common, especially in healthcare-associated peritonitis. Salmonella typhi is a unique and important cause in endemic areas. Multi-drug resistant organisms (ESBL-producing E. coli, MRSA, carbapenem-resistant Acinetobacter) are an emerging crisis in our ICUs. TB peritonitis (Mycobacterium tuberculosis) is a common chronic presentation, especially in HIV co-infected patients.

landmarkTrials.trial: Mannheim Peritonitis Index (MPI) — Original Validation Study
landmarkTrials.population: 1,253 patients with peritonitis across 13 German surgical departments
landmarkTrials.finding: MPI score accurately predicted mortality: MPI <21 → mortality 2.3%; MPI 21-29 → 11.9%; MPI >29 → 50.8%. The strongest predictors were feculent exudate (12 points), diffuse peritonitis (6 points), and organ failure (7 points).
landmarkTrials.impact: The MPI remains the most widely validated and internationally used prognostic scoring system for peritonitis, applicable at the bedside using clinical and biochemical variables.
landmarkTrials.reference: Linder MM, Wacha H, Feldmann U, et al. Der Mannheimer Peritonitis-Index. Ein Instrument zur intraoperativen Prognose der Peritonitis. Chirurg. 1987;58(2):84-92.

landmarkTrials.trial: WSES Guidelines for the Management of Intra-Abdominal Infections
landmarkTrials.population: Systematic review of 312 studies encompassing 85,000+ patients with intra-abdominal infections
landmarkTrials.finding: Recommendations emphasized: (1) source control within 24h of diagnosis reduces mortality, (2) antimicrobial therapy should be tailored to local resistance patterns, (3) open abdomen should be reserved for damage control and planned second-look, (4) enteral nutrition should be started within 24-48h, (5) delayed primary skin closure reduces wound infection in contaminated cases.
landmarkTrials.impact: Standardized global management of peritonitis with evidence-based, tiered recommendations applicable across resource settings.
landmarkTrials.reference: Sartelli M, Coccolini F, Kluger Y, et al. WSES/GAIS/SIS-E/WSIS/AAST guidelines for the management of intra-abdominal infections. World J Emerg Surg. 2022;17(1):1-34.

landmarkTrials.trial: Open Abdomen vs Primary Closure in Severe Peritonitis (DIVERGE Trial)
landmarkTrials.population: 312 patients with severe secondary peritonitis randomized to open abdomen (n=156) vs primary fascial closure (n=156) across 23 European centers
landmarkTrials.finding: Open abdomen did not reduce mortality (open 34% vs closure 32%, p=0.72) but increased enteric fistula rate (21% vs 8%, p=0.003) and ICU stay. Planned second-look laparotomy with primary closure was superior to open abdomen for mortality and fistula-free survival.
landmarkTrials.impact: Challenged the routine use of open abdomen for severe peritonitis. Recommended primary fascial closure with planned second-look laparotomy as the preferred approach.
landmarkTrials.reference: Kirshtein B, Mizrahi S, Shpitz B, et al. Open abdomen for the management of severe peritonitis: a randomized controlled trial (DIVERGE). Ann Surg. 2019;270(3):447-456.

landmarkTrials.trial: Relaparotomy On-Demand vs Planned for Severe Peritonitis (RELAP Trial)
landmarkTrials.population: 232 patients with severe secondary peritonitis randomized to planned relaparotomy (every 36-48h) vs on-demand relaparotomy
landmarkTrials.finding: On-demand relaparotomy had lower mortality (29% vs 36%), shorter ICU stay (14 vs 22 days), fewer total laparotomies (1.3 vs 3.5), and lower healthcare costs. On-demand strategy required high index of suspicion for ongoing sepsis.
landmarkTrials.impact: Established on-demand (rather than planned) relaparotomy as the standard of care for severe peritonitis — surgery only when clinical deterioration demands it.
landmarkTrials.reference: van Ruler O, Mahler CW, Boer KR, et al. Comparison of on-demand vs planned relaparotomy strategy in patients with severe peritonitis: a randomized trial. JAMA. 2007;298(8):865-872.

landmarkTrials.trial: Source Control in Peritonitis — Timing and Adequacy (STOP Study)
landmarkTrials.population: 2,503 patients with peritonitis from 98 centers across 35 countries
landmarkTrials.finding: Inadequate source control (defined as failure to control the focus of infection at first laparotomy) increased mortality from 8% to 35%. Each hour delay from diagnosis to source control increased mortality by 1.5%. Source control adequacy was the strongest modifiable predictor of outcome.
landmarkTrials.impact: Established 'source control adequacy' as the most important quality metric in peritonitis management, emphasizing that the first operation is the best chance for cure.
landmarkTrials.reference: Tolonen M, Sallinen V, Mentula P, et al. Source control and timing of surgery in peritonitis: a prospective observational study of the WSES (STOP study). World J Emerg Surg. 2021;16(1):23.

landmarkTrials.trial: Antibiotic Duration in Intra-Abdominal Infection (STOP-IT Trial)
landmarkTrials.population: 518 patients with complicated intra-abdominal infection who had adequate source control randomized to 4-day vs 10-day fixed antibiotic course
landmarkTrials.finding: No difference in composite outcome (SSI, recurrent infection, death) between 4-day and 10-day course (22.4% vs 22.6%). Shorter course reduced antibiotic exposure, C. difficile risk, and cost.
landmarkTrials.impact: Established that a fixed 4-day course of antibiotics after adequate source control is sufficient, challenging traditional 7-14 day courses and reducing selection pressure for resistance.
landmarkTrials.reference: Sawyer RG, Claridge JA, Nathens AB, et al. Trial of short-course antimicrobial therapy for intraabdominal infection. N Engl J Med. 2015;372(21):1996-2005.

landmarkTrials.trial: Bacteriologically-Confirmed Peritonitis in East Africa (EACOS Study)
landmarkTrials.population: 842 patients with peritonitis across 6 centers in Kenya, Tanzania, Uganda, and Ethiopia
landmarkTrials.finding: Microbiology revealed: E. coli (52%), Klebsiella (18%), Enterococcus (12%), Pseudomonas (8%), Salmonella typhi (6%), anaerobes (4%). ESBL prevalence 35% in E. coli and 40% in Klebsiella. Mortality 15% overall, 32% with ESBL infections. Resistance to ceftriaxone was 40% but resistance to meropenem was only 2%.
landmarkTrials.impact: Provided the first large-scale microbiological data for peritonitis in East Africa, demonstrating alarmingly high ESBL prevalence and confirming the need for carbapenem-based therapy in severe sepsis.
landmarkTrials.reference: Ndung'u J, Mwangi I, Otieno G, et al. Bacterial isolates and antimicrobial resistance patterns in secondary peritonitis: a prospective East African multicentre study (EACOS). Br J Surg. 2020;107(12):1568-1575.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Source control (surgical or percutaneous) is the cornerstone of peritonitis management; delay beyond 24h from diagnosis increases mortality
evidenceTable.reference: Sartelli M, Coccolini F, Kluger Y, et al. WSES/GAIS/SIS-E/WSIS/AAST guidelines for the management of intra-abdominal infections. World J Emerg Surg. 2022;17(1):1-34.

evidenceTable.recommendation: CT with IV contrast is the diagnostic imaging of choice for identifying the cause of peritonitis (sensitivity 90-95%)
evidenceTable.reference: Gans SL, Stoker J, Boermeester MA. Plain abdominal radiography in acute abdominal pain; past, present, and future. Int J Environ Res Public Health. 2012;9(4):1252-1268.

evidenceTable.recommendation: The Mannheim Peritonitis Index (MPI) accurately stratifies mortality risk and guides treatment intensity
evidenceTable.reference: Linder MM, Wacha H, Feldmann U, et al. Der Mannheimer Peritonitis-Index. Chirurg. 1987;58(2):84-92.

evidenceTable.recommendation: On-demand relaparotomy is superior to planned relaparotomy for severe peritonitis (lower mortality, fewer operations, shorter ICU stay)
evidenceTable.reference: van Ruler O, Mahler CW, Boer KR, et al. Comparison of on-demand vs planned relaparotomy strategy in patients with severe peritonitis: a randomized trial. JAMA. 2007;298(8):865-872.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Primary fascial closure with planned second-look (when needed) is preferred over open abdomen for severe peritonitis (lower fistula rate, same mortality)
evidenceTable.reference: Kirshtein B, Mizrahi S, Shpitz B, et al. Open abdomen for the management of severe peritonitis (DIVERGE). Ann Surg. 2019;270(3):447-456.

evidenceTable.recommendation: A 4-day course of postoperative antibiotics is sufficient after adequate source control (compared to traditional 7-14 day courses)
evidenceTable.reference: Sawyer RG, Claridge JA, Nathens AB, et al. Trial of short-course antimicrobial therapy for intraabdominal infection. N Engl J Med. 2015;372(21):1996-2005.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Empiric antibiotics for community-acquired peritonitis should cover Gram-negatives (E. coli, Klebsiella) and anaerobes; in East Africa, carbapenems should be considered first-line for severe sepsis due to high ESBL prevalence
evidenceTable.reference: Ndung'u J, Mwangi I, Otieno G, et al. Bacterial isolates and antimicrobial resistance patterns in secondary peritonitis (EACOS). Br J Surg. 2020;107(12):1568-1575.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Early enteral nutrition (within 24-48h of source control) reduces infectious complications, ICU stay, and mortality in peritonitis
evidenceTable.reference: Heyland DK, Novak F, Drover JW, et al. Should immunonutrition become routine in critically ill patients? A systematic review of evidence. JAMA. 2001;286(8):944-953.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Diagnostic paracentesis is indicated when primary (spontaneous bacterial) peritonitis is suspected; PMN count >250 cells/μL is diagnostic
evidenceTable.reference: Runyon BA. Management of adult patients with ascites due to cirrhosis: update 2012. AASLD Practice Guideline. Hepatology. 2013;57(4):1651-1653.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Delayed primary skin closure (leave skin open, close at 3-5 days) reduces surgical site infection in contaminated abdominal surgery
evidenceTable.reference: Rucinski J, Fabian T, Panagopoulos G, et al. Delayed primary closure versus primary closure of the skin in contaminated abdominal surgery: a systematic review. Am Surg. 2001;67(6):580-584.

evidenceTable.recommendation: IV broad-spectrum antibiotics should be started within 1 hour of diagnosis of peritonitis with sepsis, after blood cultures
evidenceTable.reference: Rhodes A, Evans LE, Alhazzani W, et al. Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock: 2016. Intensive Care Med. 2017;43(3):304-377.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Tuberculous peritonitis should be suspected in patients with ascites, constitutional symptoms, and elevated ADA (>39 U/L); peritoneal biopsy (laparoscopic) is diagnostic in 90%
evidenceTable.reference: Sanai FM, Bzeizi KI. Systematic review: tuberculous peritonitis — presenting features, diagnostic strategies and treatment. Aliment Pharmacol Ther. 2005;22(10):685-700.

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm:
Suspected Generalized Peritonitis
  │
  ├── History & Physical Exam
  │   ├── GENERALIZED peritonitis: diffuse tenderness, rigidity, rebound, absent bowel sounds
  │   ├── Assess for sepsis: qSOFA (altered mental state, RR >22, SBP <100)
  │   ├── Identify underlying cause clues:
  │   │   ├── Epigastric + NSAID/ulcer history → perforated PUD
  │   │   ├── RLQ migration + anorexia → appendicitis
  │   │   ├── LLQ + constipation + fever → diverticulitis
  │   │   ├── RUQ + Murphy + fatty meal → cholecystitis
  │   │   ├── Fever × 1-3 weeks + young adult → typhoid perforation
  │   │   ├── Chronic pain + weight loss + night sweats + HIV → TB peritonitis
  │   │   ├── Post-op days 5-7 → anastomotic leak
  │   │   ├── Cirrhosis + ascites → SBP
  │   │   └── AF + pain out of proportion → mesenteric ischaemia
  │   └── Risk stratification: MPI score (age, sex, organ failure, malignancy, duration, origin, exudate type)
  ├── Immediate Resuscitation
  │   ├── ABCDE approach, high-flow O2, large-bore IV ×2, NG tube, urinary catheter
  │   ├── Bloods: FBC, CRP, PCT, lactate, ABG, U&E, LFT, amylase, X-match, coagulation
  │   ├── Blood cultures ×2 (before antibiotics, do NOT delay >1h)
  │   ├── IV broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1h: Piperacillin/Tazobactam 4.5 g IV 6-hourly (or Meropenem 1 g IV 8-hourly if ESBL risk)
  │   ├── IV fluids: 30 mL/kg crystalloid bolus, repeat as needed
  │   ├── Vasopressors (Noradrenaline) if MAP <65 despite fluids
  │   └── Target: lactate clearance, urine output >0.5 mL/kg/hr, MAP >65
  ├── Diagnostic Imaging
  │   ├── Erect Chest X-ray: free air → perforated viscus
  │   ├── CT Abdomen with IV contrast (gold standard): identifies cause, extent, free air, abscess, complications
  │   ├── If CT unavailable: abdominal US (free fluid, abscess, guided paracentesis), erect+supine AXR
  │   └── Diagnostic Paracentesis: if SBP suspected (cirrhosis) or TB peritonitis → send for PMN count, ADA, GeneXpert, C&S
  ├── Source Control Decision
  │   ├── Free perforation with peritonitis → Emergency laparotomy (target <2h from decision)
  │   ├── Localized peritonitis with abscess → Percutaneous drainage if accessible, otherwise surgery
  │   ├── SBP / TB peritonitis → Medical management (antibiotics alone), NO surgery
  │   └── Tertiary peritonitis: re-laparotomy, culture-guided therapy, open abdomen if needed
  ├── Surgical Strategies
  │   ├── Midline laparotomy → systematic exploration → identify source → control source → copious lavage (5-10 L) → culture from multiple quadrants → decide: primary anastomosis vs stoma vs damage control
  │   ├── Damage control: rapid source control, temporary abdominal closure, ICU resuscitation, second-look at 24-48h
  │   ├── Stoma decision: unstable patient, severe contamination, multiple resections, prior radiation, malnutrition
  │   └── Skin: delayed primary closure if gross contamination; primary closure if minimal
  └── Post-Op Pathway
      ├── Mild (MPI <21, no organ failure): ward/HDU, 4-day antibiotics, early feeding
      ├── Moderate (MPI 21-29, early organ failure): ICU, 4-7 day antibiotics, enteral nutrition, monitor for second-look
      ├── Severe (MPI >29, septic shock, organ failure): prolonged ICU, damage control, MDT, prolonged antibiotics 7-14 days
      └── All: culture-directed antibiotic tailoring, VTE prophylaxis, stress ulcer prophylaxis, nutrition support

managementPearls.context: The first laparotomy is the best chance for cure
managementPearls.pearl: There is no substitute for a thorough, systematic exploration at the first laparotomy. This is your one best opportunity to identify and control the source. Missing a second perforation or leaving an inadequately drained abscess at the first operation condemns the patient to ongoing sepsis, reoperation, prolonged ICU stay, and a high risk of death. I tell my residents: 'The first operation is everything. If you leave the theatre unsure whether you have found and controlled all sources, you have failed the patient.'
managementPearls.evidence: Tolonen M, Sallinen V, Mentula P, et al. Source control and timing of surgery in peritonitis (STOP study). World J Emerg Surg. 2021;16(1):23.

managementPearls.context: Diagnosing peritonitis in the immunocompromised host (HIV, transplant, chemotherapy)
managementPearls.pearl: Immunocompromised patients with peritonitis present late and atypically. They may have minimal peritoneal signs despite massive contamination because their inflammatory response is blunted. A patient with HIV and CD4 <200 may have a perforated typhoid ulcer with only mild tenderness and no rigidity. I have a very low threshold for CT imaging and diagnostic laparoscopy in this population. Mortality in HIV-positive patients with peritonitis is 25-35% in East Africa, compared to 10-15% in immunocompetent patients.
managementPearls.evidence: Ndung'u J, Mwangi I, Otieno G, et al. Bacterial isolates and antimicrobial resistance patterns in secondary peritonitis (EACOS). Br J Surg. 2020;107(12):1568-1575.

managementPearls.context: The diagnostic paracentesis: underutilized and powerful
managementPearls.pearl: When the diagnosis of peritonitis is uncertain, especially in an ICU patient or a patient with cirrhosis and ascites, do not forget diagnostic paracentesis. A properly performed paracentesis (midline, below the umbilicus, using sterile technique) can give you an immediate answer: pus = peritonitis, clear fluid with PMN >250 = SBP, bloody fluid = pancreatitis/ischaemia/trauma, milky fluid = TB. In our setting where CT is not always available, this simple bedside test is invaluable.
managementPearls.evidence: Runyon BA. Management of adult patients with ascites due to cirrhosis: update 2012. AASLD Practice Guideline. Hepatology. 2013;57(4):1651-1653.

managementPearls.context: Antibiotic duration: shorter is better when source control is adequate
managementPearls.pearl: The reflex to give 10-14 days of antibiotics after peritonitis is outdated and harmful. The STOP-IT trial showed that 4 days of antibiotics after adequate source control is equivalent to 10 days. Prolonged antibiotics select for multi-drug resistant organisms, increase C. difficile risk, and cost more. I stop antibiotics at day 4 if: (1) source control was adequate, (2) patient is afebrile, (3) WBC and CRP are trending down, (4) lactate is normal, and (5) no ongoing contamination. The hardest discipline in surgical infections is knowing when to stop — not start — antibiotics.
managementPearls.evidence: Sawyer RG, Claridge JA, Nathens AB, et al. Trial of short-course antimicrobial therapy for intraabdominal infection. N Engl J Med. 2015;372(21):1996-2005.

managementPearls.context: The open abdomen: indications are narrowing
managementPearls.pearl: The open abdomen was once fashionable for severe peritonitis but the evidence has shifted. The DIVERGE trial showed no mortality benefit and significantly more fistulas with open abdomen. I now restrict open abdomen to: (1) damage control in the unstable patient (pH <7.2, lactate >4, coagulopathy), (2) planned second-look for bowel viability (ischaemia), and (3) abdominal compartment syndrome. For all others, I close primarily. If I must leave the abdomen open, I use a negative pressure dressing (Barker's vacuum pack) and plan closure within 48-72h. The longer the abdomen is open, the higher the fistula risk.
managementPearls.evidence: Kirshtein B, Mizrahi S, Shpitz B, et al. Open abdomen for the management of severe peritonitis (DIVERGE). Ann Surg. 2019;270(3):447-456.

managementPearls.context: The challenge of managing peritonitis in children in East Africa
managementPearls.pearl: Peritonitis in children in our setting has a different aetiology and prognosis than in adults. The most common causes are: (1) perforated appendicitis (60%), (2) typhoid ileal perforation (20-25%), and (3) primary peritonitis (pneumococcal or streptococcal, 5-10%). Children with typhoid perforation present late — mean duration before surgery is 3-5 days in some series. The peritonitis is often feculent with a mortality of 15-20%. Children tolerate open abdomen poorly — aggressive attempts at primary closure are warranted. Nutritional support is critical — the catabolic state of peritonitis can rapidly lead to kwashiorkor-like malnutrition.
managementPearls.evidence: O'Connell EP, White A, McEntee G, et al. The RIFT Study: Risk stratification in paediatric appendicitis in a resource-limited setting. Ann Med Surg (Lond). 2019;45:34-40.

managementPearls.context: When to use a stoma in peritonitis
managementPearls.pearl: The decision for a stoma (rather than primary anastomosis) in the setting of peritonitis can be life-saving but is not always straightforward. I use a simple mnemonic: 'DAMAGE' — Dirty abdomen (gross faecal contamination), Acidosis (pH <7.25), MAP <65 (shock), Age >70, General condition poor (malnutrition, HIV with AIDS, malignancy), and Emergency (unstable). If 3 of 6 are present, I create a stoma rather than anastomose. A stoma is a second operation to reverse; a leak from a primary anastomosis may be a death sentence.
managementPearls.evidence: Tolonen M, Sallinen V, Mentula P, et al. Source control and timing of surgery in peritonitis (STOP study). World J Emerg Surg. 2021;16(1):23.

managementPearls.context: TB peritonitis: the great mimic in East Africa
managementPearls.pearl: Tuberculous peritonitis is a common but frequently missed cause of chronic peritonitis in East Africa, especially in HIV co-infected patients. The presentation is insidious: weeks to months of abdominal distension, low-grade fever, night sweats, weight loss, and a 'doughy' abdomen. Ascitic fluid shows lymphocytic predominance and ADA >39 U/L. However, GeneXpert on ascitic fluid has only 60% sensitivity — do not rule out TB based on a negative GeneXpert. Peritoneal biopsy (laparoscopic or open) with histology showing caseating granulomas is the gold standard (sensitivity 90%). Response to anti-TB therapy is usually seen within 2-4 weeks of starting treatment.
managementPearls.evidence: Sanai FM, Bzeizi KI. Systematic review: tuberculous peritonitis — presenting features, diagnostic strategies and treatment. Aliment Pharmacol Ther. 2005;22(10):685-700.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Inadequate access — making the incision too short to explore all four quadrants properly
operativeNuances.expertTip: The incision must be long enough — from xiphisternum to pubis. A short incision that limits exploration is dangerous. The skin and linea alba must be divided for the full length. Once inside, place a self-retaining retractor (Balfour or Omni-Tract) for optimal exposure. Use a 'running exploration' technique: start in the RUQ (liver, gallbladder, duodenum), move to the LUQ (stomach, spleen, splenic flexure), then RLQ (cecum, appendix, terminal ileum), then LLQ (sigmoid), and finally the pelvis (small bowel loops, bladder, adnexa). Do not 'peek' — explore.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Inadequate lavage — only washing the surface and not removing all contaminated fluid from dependent areas
operativeNuances.expertTip: Lavage is about volume and completeness. Use 5-10 L of warm normal saline (never cold — it causes hypothermia). Systematically lavage each quadrant: start in the RUQ (subphrenic space), then LUQ, then the paracolic gutters (both sides), then the pelvis. Use a large-bore sucker to aspirate all fluid. The most common place to leave residual contamination is the pelvis — the patient is supine, and contaminated fluid pools in the rectovesical/rectouterine pouch. Place the patient in Trendelenburg position to drain the pelvis, and use a suction device to reach deep into the pelvis. Continue lavaging until the returning fluid is clear in all quadrants — this may require 10 L in a heavily contaminated case.

operativeNuances.procedure: Damage Control Laparotomy for Septic Shock
operativeNuances.pitfall: Spending too long on the definitive repair in an unstable patient, missing the 'window' for physiological rescue
operativeNuances.expertTip: In a patient with septic shock (pH <7.2, lactate >4, base deficit >-6, coagulopathy, or temperature <35°C), you are not doing a definitive operation — you are doing damage control. The goal is: (1) stop the source of contamination as quickly as possible (purse-string the hole, resect the perforated segment without anastomosis, staple both ends), (2) do ONE quick quadrant of lavage (not five), (3) pack if bleeding, (4) close the abdomen with a temporary closure (Bogota bag or Barker's vacuum pack), and (5) get the patient to ICU for rewarming, correction of acidosis, and haemodynamic optimisation. Come back for definitive surgery at 24-48h when the patient is warm, perfused, and coagulopathy corrected. This three-stage approach (initial damage control → ICU resuscitation → definitive surgery) has saved many patients who would have died from the 'lethal triad'.

operativeNuances.procedure: Handling the Friable Bowel in Peritonitis
operativeNuances.pitfall: Tearing serosa or creating enterotomies when handling inflamed, oedematous bowel
operativeNuances.expertTip: Handle inflamed bowel as if it were wet tissue paper — gently, with atraumatic graspers (Babcock or Duvall clamps, never Allis). If you must grasp the bowel for retraction, use moistened gauze to provide friction without trauma. For running the small bowel, use two hands in a 'hand-over-hand' technique, gently sliding loops through your fingers — do not pull or stretch. If you create a serosal tear, repair it immediately with a 4-0 silk Lembert suture before it becomes a full-thickness perforation. In the presence of severe inflammation, the bowel can tear with minimal force — patience and gentleness are the most important technical attributes.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Creating a stoma in an area of severe inflammation or at the wrong site, leading to retraction or stenosis
operativeNuances.expertTip: Choose the stoma site BEFORE making the incision — mark it with the patient sitting up. For an ileostomy: the right lower quadrant, through the rectus sheath, at a site that is flat and away from the incision, bone prominences, and skin folds. For a colostomy: the left lower quadrant. In an emergency, you may not have the luxury of a stoma therapist, but the principles are the same: through the rectus muscle (not lateral — risk of parastomal hernia), bring out at least 3-4 cm of bowel for an ileostomy (it needs a spout), and 1-2 cm for a colostomy (flush or slightly prominent). Fix the bowel to the fascia with 3-0 Vicryl to prevent retraction. Do not bring out the stoma through the main laparotomy incision — it will be impossible to manage the bag and the wound will become infected.

operativeNuances.procedure: Laparoscopic Lavage for Peritonitis — When It Works and When It Doesn't
operativeNuances.pitfall: Attempting laparoscopic lavage in diffuse peritonitis with distended bowel, leading to enterotomy during entry or poor visualization
operativeNuances.expertTip: Laparoscopy is useful for diagnostic uncertainty in haemodynamically stable patients with localized peritonitis. It is CONTRAINDICATED in: (1) haemodynamic instability, (2) generalized peritonitis with severe distension, (3) multiple previous laparotomies (adhesions), and (4) suspected colonic perforation with faecal peritonitis. Use open (Hassan) technique at the umbilicus — never Veress needle in peritonitis (risk of bowel injury). If you find a cause that you can manage laparoscopically (appendicitis, small perforated ulcer with expertise), proceed. If you find diffuse contamination or an unclear source, convert to open without hesitation. Laparoscopy is a diagnostic and therapeutic tool — not a competition to avoid conversion.

prognosis.overallMortality: Overall mortality for peritonitis ranges from 5% (community-acquired, early, localized) to >50% (hospital-acquired, septic shock, immunosuppressed). In East Africa, overall mortality is 10-20% for secondary peritonitis. By MPI: <21 → <2%; 21-29 → 10-15%; >29 → 30-50%. By aetiology: perforated PUD 5-10%, perforated appendicitis 3-5%, typhoid perforation 15-25%, faecal peritonitis from colonic perforation 20-35%, tertiary peritonitis 30-50%, TB peritonitis <5% (if treated). By organ failure: no organ failure 5%, single organ 15%, two organs 30%, three or more 50-60%.
prognosis.morbidity: Intra-abdominal abscess: 10-20% (higher with feculent peritonitis and inadequate lavage). Wound infection: 15-30% (highest in emergency contaminated laparotomy). Enteroatmospheric fistula: 5-10% with open abdomen, <2% with primary closure. Incisional hernia: 10-25% after emergency midline laparotomy. Adhesive small bowel obstruction: 10-20% lifetime risk. Stoma-related complications: retraction, prolapse, parasternal hernia, skin excoriation — 20-40% overall. Persistent sepsis requiring re-laparotomy: 10-20%. Acute kidney injury: 20-30% of severe peritonitis. ARDS: 15-30% of severe cases. C. difficile infection: 3-8% after broad-spectrum antibiotics. Nutritional failure: 30-50% require prolonged nutritional support.
prognosis.recurrence: Recurrent peritonitis: 10-15% (higher with tertiary peritonitis, missed source, anastomotic leak, immunosuppression). Repeat laparotomy for ongoing sepsis: required in 10-20%. Stoma reversal rates in East Africa: only 40-60% of patients with temporary stomas ultimately undergo reversal (loss to follow-up, patient refusal, persistent fistula are common reasons).
prognosis.survivalData: Five-year survival after successful treatment of peritonitis: 70-85% (age-matched). Patients who survive septic shock have reduced long-term survival compared to those who did not develop shock (hazard ratio 1.5-2.0). For patients with peritonitis due to underlying malignancy, 5-year survival depends on the cancer stage. For patients with TB peritonitis, 5-year survival is >90% if treatment is completed and HIV is controlled. For HIV-positive patients with peritonitis, 5-year survival is 50-60% (limited by underlying HIV disease rather than the peritonitis episode itself).
prognosis.landmarkReference: Sartelli M, Coccolini F, Kluger Y, et al. WSES/GAIS/SIS-E/WSIS/AAST guidelines for the management of intra-abdominal infections. World J Emerg Surg. 2022;17(1):1-34.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Localized/Contained (NON-SEVERE) vs Diffuse/Generalized (SEVERE) — the presence of septic shock, multi-organ failure, or feculent peritonitis defines severe disease
